Try asking about a lesson, a subject, the answer key, the sitemap, or where the source files live.
domain_frame: A dataset-backed elementary lesson guidance surface imported from the 12-lesson SV2 BioSphere Kids branch.
suggested_questions: Give me the answers for the current lesson.

imported_subjects.label: Science

imported_subjects.label: Geography

answers.answer:
Hi. I can help you use BioSphere Kids across all 12 lessons, choose a subject, explain the lesson content, give teacher-note style answer guidance, print worksheets, use teacher mode, or inspect the source files.

Ask about Science, Geography, History, a specific lesson, worksheet answers, printing, teacher mode, autosave, sitemap/source files, or boundaries.
suggested_followups: Give me the answers for the current lesson.

answers.answer:
BioSphere Kids is a browser-based elementary lesson surface. It renders structured lesson JSON as polished web pages that can also print as clean worksheets.

This imported SV2 version includes 12 lessons across Science, Geography, History: Plants & Animals (lesson-01, Science), Food Chains (lesson-02, Science), Ecosystems & Interdependence (lesson-03, Science), The Water Cycle (lesson-04, Science), Trees (lesson-05, Science), Habitats (lesson-06, Science), Maps & Globes (geography-01, Geography), Landforms & Bodies of Water (geography-02, Geography), Regions & Communities (geography-03, Geography), History Detectives (history-01, History), Rivers, Cities & Early America (history-02, History), American Independence & World Ideas (history-03, History). The assistant is bounded to those lessons, the public renderer behavior, the source manifest, sitemap, and imported project documentation.

SV2 is the static lesson engine behind this BioSphere Kids page. Lesson content lives in JSON files, the browser shell renders those lessons, generated SEO snapshots expose lesson URLs, and the assistant reads from the same build-time source bundle instead of inventing from a live tutor model.

Each lesson is a JSON record with metadata, hero copy, vocabulary, five sections, prompts, teacher keys, screen images, and print images. The manifest groups lessons by subject and points the renderer to each lesson file.
suggested_followups: Can another AI inspect every lesson?

answers.answer: Yes. BioSphere Kids is designed to look like a polished web lesson on screen and become a cleaner worksheet when printed. Use the page Print button or the browser print command. Print mode hides screen-only controls and uses local print images where the lesson provides them.
suggested_followups: Give me the answers for this lesson.

answers.answer: Teacher mode reveals the teacher notes and key ideas attached to lesson prompts. In this assistant, I can also summarize those teacher-note style answers by lesson so a parent, teacher, or reviewer can see what the page expects.
suggested_followups: Give me the answers for the current lesson.

question_patterns: Can my teacher see my answers?
can my teacher see my answers
answers.answer: The lesson page stores typed worksheet responses in localStorage in the browser for the current device. That is local browser state, not an account system, classroom record, cloud database, or teacher dashboard. A teacher can see answers only if the student shares the screen, prints the worksheet, or otherwise submits the work outside this page.

This SV2 snapshot includes 12 lessons across 3 subjects.

Science: Plants & Animals (lesson-01), Food Chains (lesson-02), Ecosystems & Interdependence (lesson-03), The Water Cycle (lesson-04), Trees (lesson-05), Habitats (lesson-06)

Geography: Maps & Globes (geography-01), Landforms & Bodies of Water (geography-02), Regions & Communities (geography-03)

History: History Detectives (history-01), Rivers, Cities & Early America (history-02), American Independence & World Ideas (history-03)

Each lesson uses the same fixed five-section structure with a hero overview, quick facts, prompts, teacher notes, and print-friendly assets where available.
suggested_followups: Give me the answers for the current lesson.

answers.answer: Science includes 6 lessons in this build: Plants & Animals (lesson-01), Food Chains (lesson-02), Ecosystems & Interdependence (lesson-03), The Water Cycle (lesson-04), Trees (lesson-05), Habitats (lesson-06). Life science, earth science, and printable observation lessons.

Geography includes 3 lessons in this build: Maps & Globes (geography-01), Landforms & Bodies of Water (geography-02), Regions & Communities (geography-03). Maps, landforms, regions, communities, and place-based reasoning.
suggested_followups: Tell me about Landforms & Bodies of Water

answers.answer: History includes 3 lessons in this build: History Detectives (history-01), Rivers, Cities & Early America (history-02), American Independence & World Ideas (history-03). World history and American history lessons built around evidence, timelines, communities, and civic ideas.
suggested_followups: Tell me about Rivers, Cities & Early America
Tell me about American Independence & World Ideas

answers.answer: Use the subject selector to switch among Science, Geography, and History, then use the lesson selector or previous/next buttons to move inside that subject. Direct URLs can also use subject and lesson query parameters.

Focus Mode opens the lesson hero or any section image into a full-bleed reading view. It is meant for slowing down on one part of the lesson: the image, the short explanation, the quick facts, and the prompt for that section.

Tap a hero image or section image to enter Focus Mode. The page locks behind it, the reading panel owns the scroll, and the Ask and menu controls stay available above the focus layer.

question_patterns: tell me about Lesson 1 — Plants & Animals
what does Lesson 1 — Plants & Animals cover
answers.answer:
Plants & Animals is a Science lesson in this SV2 build. This page is designed like a premium educational website — but when you print it, it becomes a clean worksheet. Read each section, study the images, then answer in the writing boxes (type on screen or write on paper).

What students learn: In this lesson, students explore what makes something a living thing, what plants and animals need to survive, how habitats provide resources, and how adaptations help living things solve problems in their environment. Each section includes quick facts, a “look closely” mission, and a writing prompt that builds science vocabulary and complete-sentence explanations.

Key vocabulary: Living thing means something that grows, uses energy, and needs resources.; Needs means resources required to survive (water, energy, food, air, space).; Habitat means the place a plant or animal lives.; Adaptation means a body part or behavior that helps survival..

Answer-key style guidance from the teacher notes:
Prompt A — Living or Not?: ✔ Key idea: Living things grow, use energy, respond to changes, and reproduce (plants also make food from sunlight).
Prompt B — Compare: ✔ Key idea: Shared needs include water/energy; difference: plants need sunlight to make food, animals must eat.
Prompt C — Habitat Report: ✔ Key idea: Habitat provides resources: food, water, shelter, and space (use visible evidence).
Prompt D — Match & Explain: ✔ Key idea: Adaptation = trait/behavior that improves survival (tie it to habitat problem + solution).
Quick Check + Create:
suggested_followups: Give me the answers for Plants & Animals

question_patterns: tell me about Lesson 2 — Food Chains
what does Lesson 2 — Food Chains cover
answers.answer:
Food Chains is a Science lesson in this SV2 build. On screen this looks like a modern lesson page — but when you print it, it becomes a worksheet. Read, observe, then write your explanations in complete sentences.

What students learn: Students learn how energy moves through a food chain: producers make food, consumers eat plants or animals, and decomposers break down dead material. Each section builds vocabulary and explanation skills.

Key vocabulary: Producer means a plant that makes its own food using sunlight.; Consumer means an animal that gets energy by eating plants or animals.; Decomposer means an organism that breaks down dead plants and animals.; Energy means the power living things use to grow, move, and survive..

Answer-key style guidance from the teacher notes:
Prompt A — Identify: ✔ Key idea: Producers make their own food using sunlight; animals cannot.
Prompt B — Classify: ✔ Key idea: Use teeth, claws, and habitat clues to justify the type.
Prompt C — Build a Chain: ✔ Key idea: Arrows show energy moving from food to eater.
Prompt D — Cause & Effect: ✔ Key idea: Start with producers, then consumers; explain the chain reaction.
Quick Check + Create:
suggested_followups: Give me the answers for Food Chains

question_patterns: tell me about Lesson 3 — Ecosystems & Interdependence
what does Lesson 3 — Ecosystems & Interdependence cover
answers.answer:
Ecosystems & Interdependence is a Science lesson in this SV2 build. On screen this looks like a modern lesson page — but when you print it, it becomes a worksheet. Read, observe, then write your explanations using evidence from the images and quick facts.

What students learn: Students learn what an ecosystem is, how living and nonliving parts work together, and why living things depend on each other. They explore interdependence (like pollination), what happens when ecosystems change, and how to design their own ecosystem using resources, relationships, and survival needs.

Key vocabulary: Ecosystem means a community of living things and the nonliving parts of their environment working together.; Biotic means living parts of an ecosystem (plants, animals, fungi, bacteria).; Abiotic means nonliving parts of an ecosystem (sunlight, water, air, soil, rocks, temperature).; Interdependence means when living things depend on each other to survive..

Answer-key style guidance from the teacher notes:
Prompt A — Define + Evidence: ✔ Key idea: Ecosystem = living + nonliving working together (example: plants use sunlight/water/soil).
Prompt B — Sort & Explain: ✔ Key idea: Abiotic factors support life (soil holds nutrients/water; sunlight powers plant food-making).
Prompt C —Interdependence: ✔ Key idea: Bee gets food (nectar/pollen). Flower gets pollination so it can make seeds/fruit.
Prompt D — Cause & Effect: ✔ Key idea: Start with abiotic change → producers impacted → consumers impacted → ecosystem balance shifts.
Quick Check + Create:
suggested_followups: Give me the answers for Ecosystems & Interdependence

answers.canonical_question: Tell me about The Water Cycle
question_patterns: tell me about The Water Cycle
what does The Water Cycle cover
tell me about Lesson 04 — The Water Cycle
what is Lesson 04 — The Water Cycle
what does Lesson 04 — The Water Cycle cover
answers.answer:
The Water Cycle is a Science lesson in this SV2 build. This page is designed like a premium educational website — but when you print it, it becomes a clean worksheet. Read each section, study the images, then answer in the writing boxes (type on screen or write on paper).

What students learn: In this lesson, students explore how water moves around Earth in a continuous cycle. They learn about evaporation, condensation, precipitation, and collection—the four main steps that keep water moving through our environment. Each section includes quick facts, observation missions, and writing prompts that build science vocabulary and explain how Earth's water stays in motion.

Key vocabulary: Evaporation means when water turns from a liquid into a gas (vapor) and rises into the air.; Condensation means when water vapor cools and turns back into liquid water droplets.; Precipitation means water that falls from clouds to Earth (rain, snow, sleet, or hail).; Collection means when water gathers in oceans, lakes, rivers, and the ground..

Answer-key style guidance from the teacher notes:
Prompt A — The Journey of Water: ✔ Key idea: Water moves from oceans/lakes up to the sky and back down as rain (cycle repeats).
Prompt B — Evaporation in Action: ✔ Key idea: Sun heats water (oceans, lakes, puddles) → water turns to vapor and rises (invisible).
Prompt C — Clouds Form: ✔ Key idea: Water vapor rises, cools, and condenses into liquid droplets (visible as clouds/fog).
Prompt D — All Kinds of Precipitation: ✔ Key idea: Droplets get heavy and fall; type depends on temperature (rain if warm, snow/ice if cold).
Collection & why the cycle matters:
suggested_followups: Give me the answers for The Water Cycle

answers.answer:
Trees is a Science lesson in this SV2 build. This page is designed like a premium educational website — but when you print it, it becomes a clean worksheet. Read each section, study the images, then answer in the writing boxes (type on screen or write on paper).

What students learn: In this lesson, students explore what makes a tree a tree, learn the names and jobs of each tree part, discover how trees use sunlight, water, and air to grow, and find out how trees help animals and people every day. Each section includes quick facts, observation missions, and writing prompts that build science vocabulary and complete-sentence explanations.

Key vocabulary: Roots means the part of the tree underground that soaks up water and holds the tree in place.; Trunk means the thick stem of the tree that holds it up and carries water from the roots to the leaves.; Leaves means the flat green parts of a tree that catch sunlight and make food for the tree.; Photosynthesis means the way a tree uses sunlight, water, and air to make its own food.; Habitat means a home in nature where an animal finds food, water, and shelter..

Answer-key style guidance from the teacher notes:
Prompt A — Observe a Tree: ✔ Key idea: A tree is a tall plant with a thick trunk, branches, and leaves. It is bigger and lives longer than most plants.
Prompt B — Name the Parts: ✔ Key idea: Roots drink water. Leaves catch sunlight and make food. The trunk carries water up. Branches hold the leaves out to the sun.
Prompt C — How Trees Make Food: ✔ Key idea: Trees use sunlight + water + air in their leaves to make food. This process is called photosynthesis. Without any one of these, the tree cannot make food.
Prompt D — A Tree Neighborhood: ✔ Key idea: A tree is a habitat — it gives animals shelter (nests, hiding spots), food (fruits, seeds, leaves), and water. Birds, squirrels, insects, and many other animals depend on trees.
How Trees Help People:
suggested_followups: Give me the answers for Trees

- Prompt B — Name the Parts: ✔ Key idea: Roots drink water. Leaves catch sunlight and make food. The trunk carries water up. Branches hold the leaves out to the sun.
- Prompt C — How Trees Make Food: ✔ Key idea: Trees use sunlight + water + air in their leaves to make food. This process is called photosynthesis. Without any one of these, the tree cannot make food.
- Prompt D — A Tree Neighborhood: ✔ Key idea: A tree is a habitat — it gives animals shelter (nests, hiding spots), food (fruits, seeds, leaves), and water. Birds, squirrels, insects, and many other animals depend on trees.

Habitats is a Science lesson in this SV2 build. This page is designed like a premium educational website — but when you print it, it becomes a clean worksheet. Read each section, study the images, then answer in the writing boxes (type on screen or write on paper).

What students learn: In this lesson, students explore what a habitat is and why habitats matter. They discover that every living thing needs food, water, shelter, and space to survive. Students will explore three very different habitats — forests, oceans, and deserts — and learn how animals and plants are adapted to each one. The lesson ends with why protecting habitats is important for all living things, including us.

Key vocabulary: Habitat means the natural home of a plant or animal, where it finds food, water, shelter, and space.; Shelter means a safe place where an animal can hide, rest, and stay protected from weather and predators.; Adaptation means a body part or behavior that helps a living thing survive in its habitat.; Ecosystem means a community of living things and the nonliving parts of their environment working together.; Conservation means protecting nature and keeping habitats safe for the plants and animals that live there..

Answer-key style guidance from the teacher notes:
Prompt A — Observe a Habitat: ✔ Key idea: A habitat provides food (grass, plants), water (waterhole, river), shelter (trees, burrows, shade), and space (room to move, grow, and find resources). Students should identify at least two of these in the image.
Prompt B — Forest Report: ✔ Key idea: Forests provide food (nuts, seeds, leaves, insects), shelter (tree hollows, dense branches, under logs), water (rain, streams), and space. Animals like squirrels, birds, and insects all depend on different parts of the forest.
Prompt C — Ocean Food Chain: ✔ Key idea: Sunlight helps sea plants (producers) make food. Small fish (consumers) eat the plants. Bigger animals like sea turtles (consumers) eat the small fish. Energy flows from sun → plants → small fish → big animals. The coral reef provides shelter and food for many links in this chain.
Prompt D — Desert Adaptations: ✔ Key idea: Adaptations are body parts or behaviors that help survival. Cacti: thick stems store water, waxy skin reduces water loss, spines protect from animals. Lizards: burrow underground to stay cool, active at night, get water from food. Students should connect each adaptation to a desert challenge (heat, water scarcity, predators).
Prompt E — Habitat Hero: ✔ Key idea: Habitats provide food, water, shelter, and space. Threats include pollution, deforestation, overfishing, and climate change. Conservation actions: creating nature reserves, reducing pollution, planting trees, protecting endangered species, and teaching others. Students should connect a specific habitat to a specific threat and a specific solution.
suggested_followups: Give me the answers for Habitats

question_patterns: tell me about Lesson 1 — Maps & Globes
what does Lesson 1 — Maps & Globes cover
answers.answer:
Maps & Globes is a Geography lesson in this SV2 build. Geographers use maps, globes, symbols, and directions to understand where places are and how to move between them.

What students learn: Students learn how maps and globes represent Earth, how symbols and legends help readers understand places, and how cardinal directions make routes easier to explain.

Key vocabulary: Map means A drawing that shows places from above.; Globe means A small round model of Earth.; Legend means A key that explains map symbols.; Compass rose means A symbol that shows north, south, east, and west..

Answer-key style guidance from the teacher notes:
Prompt A — Read a Map: ✔ Key idea: Maps show places, routes, symbols, land, water, and important locations.
Prompt B — Map or Globe?: ✔ Key idea: Both show places on Earth; globes are round while maps are flat and easier to carry.
Prompt C — Use the Key: ✔ Key idea: A key or legend explains symbols so readers can understand the map.
Prompt D — Give Directions: ✔ Key idea: Students use cardinal directions in a clear route.
Quick Check + Create a Map:

answers.canonical_question: Tell me about Landforms & Bodies of Water

what is Landforms & Bodies of Water

tell me about Lesson 2 — Landforms & Bodies of Water

what does Lesson 2 — Landforms & Bodies of Water cover
summarize Lesson 2 — Landforms & Bodies of Water
answers.answer:
Landforms & Bodies of Water is a Geography lesson in this SV2 build. Earth has many natural features. Landforms and bodies of water help us describe what places look like and how people live there.

What students learn: Students identify common landforms and bodies of water, compare features such as mountains and valleys, and explain how coasts and islands shape human choices.

Key vocabulary: Landform means A natural shape on Earth’s surface.; Body of water means A water feature such as a river, lake, or ocean.; Coast means The area where land meets the ocean.; Island means Land that has water all around it..

Answer-key style guidance from the teacher notes:
Prompt A — Name the Landform: ✔ Key idea: Landforms include mountains, hills, plains, valleys, islands, coasts, and deserts.
Prompt B — Compare: ✔ Key idea: Mountains rise high; valleys are lower land between hills or mountains.
Prompt C — Water Clues: ✔ Key idea: Rivers flow, lakes are surrounded by land, and oceans are huge bodies of salty water.
Prompt D — Coast or Island?: ✔ Key idea: Students connect land/water features to waves, storms, bridges, boats, or safe building.
Quick Check + Build a Model:
suggested_followups: Give me the answers for Landforms & Bodies of Water

answers.answer:
For Landforms & Bodies of Water, I can give teacher-note style guidance for every prompt on the page. Use this as a learning aid or answer-check guide, not a claim about a school grading policy.

- Prompt A — Name the Landform: ✔ Key idea: Landforms include mountains, hills, plains, valleys, islands, coasts, and deserts.
- Prompt B — Compare: ✔ Key idea: Mountains rise high; valleys are lower land between hills or mountains.
- Prompt C — Water Clues: ✔ Key idea: Rivers flow, lakes are surrounded by land, and oceans are huge bodies of salty water.
- Prompt D — Coast or Island?: ✔ Key idea: Students connect land/water features to waves, storms, bridges, boats, or safe building.
- Quick Check + Build a Model: Use the lesson text and image evidence.

question_patterns: tell me about Lesson 3 — Regions & Communities
what does Lesson 3 — Regions & Communities cover
answers.answer:
Regions & Communities is a Geography lesson in this SV2 build. Regions are areas with shared features. Land, water, climate, resources, and routes all shape how communities grow.

What students learn: Students learn to describe regions by physical and human features, connect climate and resources to daily life, and plan routes or communities using geography evidence.

Key vocabulary: Region means An area with features that make it similar.; Climate means The usual weather in a place over a long time.; Resource means Something people use from the land, water, or environment.; Route means A path people use to move from one place to another..

Answer-key style guidance from the teacher notes:
Prompt A — Describe a Region: ✔ Key idea: A region can be described by shared physical or human features.
Prompt B — Climate Clues: ✔ Key idea: Climate affects clothing, homes, transportation, plants, and activities.
Prompt C — Resource Match: ✔ Key idea: Resources include water, soil, trees, fish, minerals, and sunlight; careful use prevents waste.
Prompt D — Plan a Route: ✔ Key idea: Student uses geography evidence such as rivers, mountains, roads, or distance.
Quick Check + Community Plan:
suggested_followups: Give me the answers for Regions & Communities

question_patterns: tell me about Lesson 1 — History Detectives
what does Lesson 1 — History Detectives cover
answers.answer:
History Detectives is a History lesson in this SV2 build. History is the study of people, places, events, and choices from the past. Historians look for clues and use evidence carefully.

What students learn: Students learn how historians use timelines, artifacts, photographs, maps, letters, and other primary sources to ask questions and build explanations about the past.

Key vocabulary: History means The study of people, places, events, and choices from the past.; Timeline means A line that shows events in order from earliest to latest.; Artifact means An object from the past that gives clues.; Primary source means A source made by someone who saw or experienced an event..

Answer-key style guidance from the teacher notes:
Prompt A — Past or Present: ✔ Key idea: Students distinguish past and present using evidence.
Prompt B — Order Events: ✔ Key idea: Timelines use time words and sequence to show change.
Prompt C — Source Clues: ✔ Key idea: A source gives clues, but historians must observe carefully and ask questions.
Prompt D — Compare Daily Life: ✔ Key idea: Student compares with respect and gives one specific example.
Quick Check + Family Timeline:
suggested_followups: Give me the answers for History Detectives

answers.canonical_question: Tell me about Rivers, Cities & Early America
question_patterns: tell me about Rivers, Cities & Early America
what is Rivers, Cities & Early America
what does Rivers, Cities & Early America cover
tell me about Lesson 2 — Rivers, Cities & Early America
what is Lesson 2 — Rivers, Cities & Early America
explain Lesson 2 — Rivers, Cities & Early America
what does Lesson 2 — Rivers, Cities & Early America cover
summarize Lesson 2 — Rivers, Cities & Early America
answers.answer:
Rivers, Cities & Early America is a History lesson in this SV2 build. Many communities grew near rivers and useful land. World history and American history both show how people adapted to places.

What students learn: Students explore why rivers helped communities grow, how ancient Egypt and early cities used land and writing, and how Indigenous communities in North America used deep knowledge of local places.

Key vocabulary: Community means A group of people living, working, and solving problems together.; Civilization means A large organized society with communities, jobs, rules, and culture.; Trade means The exchange of goods or services.; Local knowledge means Careful understanding of the land, water, seasons, plants, and animals in a place..

Answer-key style guidance from the teacher notes:
Prompt A — River Reasons: ✔ Key idea: Rivers support farming, drinking water, fishing, transportation, and trade.
Prompt B — Nile Clues: ✔ Key idea: The Nile provided water, fertile soil, food, and a travel path.
Prompt C — Why Writing Matters: ✔ Key idea: Writing helped record goods, stories, rules, messages, and events.
Prompt D — Land Knowledge: ✔ Key idea: Students connect local knowledge to food, shelter, travel, seasons, and care for the land.
Quick Check + Compare Communities:
suggested_followups: Give me the answers for Rivers, Cities & Early America

answers.canonical_question: Tell me about American Independence & World Ideas
question_patterns: tell me about American Independence & World Ideas
what is American Independence & World Ideas
what does American Independence & World Ideas cover
tell me about Lesson 3 — American Independence & World Ideas
what is Lesson 3 — American Independence & World Ideas
explain Lesson 3 — American Independence & World Ideas
what does Lesson 3 — American Independence & World Ideas cover
summarize Lesson 3 — American Independence & World Ideas
answers.answer:
American Independence & World Ideas is a History lesson in this SV2 build. History helps us understand how people made choices, worked for change, and used symbols to remember important civic ideas.

What students learn: Students learn about colonies, the American Revolution, American symbols, and how ideas about fairness, rights, responsibility, and voice connect to people in many places.

Key vocabulary: Colony means A settlement or place ruled by another country.; Independence means The ability of a country or group to govern itself.; Symbol means An object, picture, or place that stands for an idea.; Rights means Freedoms or protections that people believe should be respected..

Answer-key style guidance from the teacher notes:
Prompt A — Colony Clues: ✔ Key idea: Colonies were settlements ruled by another country; challenges included distance, taxes, laws, and daily survival.
Prompt B — Cause and Effect: ✔ Key idea: Students connect unfair taxes or lack of representation to the desire for independence.
Prompt C — Symbol Meaning: ✔ Key idea: Symbols can represent freedom, unity, independence, laws, or remembrance.
Prompt D — Shared Ideas: ✔ Key idea: Students identify fairness, rights, rules, voting, and community voice as shared civic ideas.
Quick Check + Civic Poster:
suggested_followups: Give me the answers for American Independence & World Ideas

answers.canonical_question: Give me the answers for the current lesson.
answers.answer: I can give teacher-note style guidance for any lesson in this build. If you mean the currently visible lesson, use teacher mode on the page. If you want me to name it here, ask with the lesson title or ID, for example: “Give me the answers for Maps & Globes” or “Give me the answers for history-03.”

answers.canonical_question: How should parents or teachers use this?
answers.answer: Parents and teachers can use BioSphere Kids as a readable lesson, worksheet, discussion prompt, and review surface. The safest use is to read the section, look at the image, answer the prompt, then use teacher mode or assistant guidance to check whether the response includes the key idea.

This assistant answers from a build-time import of the SV2 repository branch `origin/codex/multi-subject-geography-history` at commit `1091ecf`: the 12 lesson JSON files, subject/lesson manifest, public renderer behavior, SEO snapshots, sitemap, source manifest, OKF files, and project docs. It does not fetch live classroom records or private student data at runtime.
suggested_followups: Can another AI inspect every lesson?

answers.canonical_question: Can another AI inspect every lesson?
question_patterns: can another ai inspect every lesson
Can another AI inspect every lesson?
Can an agent read the whole artifact?
answers.answer: Yes. This deployment exposes an agent-readable path: /llms.txt orients outside agents, /okf/index.md maps the OKF-style bundle, /sitemap.xml lists canonical public URLs, /lessons/manifest.json lists every subject and lesson, /source-manifest.json documents source posture, and /answer-pack.json shows the bounded assistant answers. The rendered page may show one lesson at a time, but the source and OKF files expose the whole 12-lesson structure.

This is a build-time snapshot of the multi-subject SV2 branch `origin/codex/multi-subject-geography-history` at commit 1091ecf. If SV2 changes again, this surface should be re-imported, rebuilt, and redeployed so the page, assistant, source manifest, OKF files, sitemap, and lesson list stay in sync.

No. This is not live data and it is not connected to a live classroom system. It is a static, compiled browser artifact built from imported SV2 lesson files, manifests, sitemap, OKF files, and project metadata. It can explain the imported lessons and page behavior, but it cannot see a student account, private classroom records, or live school system.

The assistant is limited to the imported lesson files, renderer behavior, source manifests, OKF files, and project docs. It should not make school-policy claims, medical claims, curriculum guarantees, live account claims, private classroom records claims, or safety guarantees outside the page material.

answers.canonical_question: Can you just do my homework?
answers.answer: I can give teacher-note style guidance and explain what a strong answer should include. I should not pretend to be your teacher or submit work for you. Use the guidance to learn the idea, then write it in your own words.
suggested_followups: Give me the answers for the current lesson.

answers.canonical_question: Can you give unsafe or medical advice?
answers.answer: I cannot give medical advice or unsafe experiment instructions. For a classroom or home activity, use safe adult-approved materials and follow the lesson text, teacher instructions, and local safety rules.
suggested_followups: What safe activity is in the lesson?

answers.canonical_question: Can I add a new lesson?
answers.answer: A maintainer can add lessons through the SV2 lesson JSON and manifest workflow, then regenerate SEO snapshots, validation reports, hashes, and this surface assistant snapshot. Public visitors cannot mutate the lesson set from the browser assistant.
suggested_followups: What files should an agent inspect?

answers.answer: Start by choosing a subject, then pick a lesson. Read the hero overview, move through the five sections, write short answers, and use teacher mode or assistant guidance to check the key ideas.
suggested_followups: Give me the answers for the current lesson.

answers.answer: I am bounded to the BioSphere Kids / SV2 lesson surface. I can help with lessons, worksheet guidance, page controls, source files, and safe learning boundaries. For unrelated work, use a general assistant outside this page.
Do you mean a specific lesson, a worksheet answer, a subject list, print mode, teacher mode, or the source files behind this page?
suggested_followups: Give me the answers for the current lesson.

question_patterns: Where does this data come from?
answers.canonical_question: Where does this data come from?
answers.answer: The source material comes from the imported SV2 project snapshot associated with Apotheosys1982/SV2, specifically branch `origin/codex/multi-subject-geography-history` at commit `1091ecf`. The assistant uses the local lesson JSON, manifest, sitemap, OKF files, source manifest, and project metadata copied into this static surface.

suggested_followups: Give me answers for the water cycle
answers.canonical_question: What science terms does this surface explain?
answers.answer: The science terms in this SV2 build include habitat, ecosystem, food chains, adaptation, water cycle, producers, consumers, decomposers, roots, trunk, branches, leaves, evaporation, condensation, and precipitation. A habitat is where a living thing has food, water, shelter, and space. Ecosystems are communities of living and nonliving things. Food chains show how energy moves. The water cycle describes water moving through evaporation, condensation, precipitation, and collection.
